title: Heidegger and Boredom in Stranger Than Paradise (1984)
subtitle: Sitting with boredom and the journey to authentic living

title: The Trial by Franz Kafka
content:
I started *The Trial* having consumed some of its inescapable influence through Bulgakov's *The Master and Margarita*, Gilliam's *Brazil*, and Scorsese's *After Hours* (all personal favorites). Going in, I already had the general gist of a man navigating an absurd bureaucratic nightmare in mind.

What surprised me is how outrageously funny the book is. It’s not overtly ludicrous like *The* *Master and Margarita* (there are no alcoholic pistol-toting cats), and Josef K. is more outwardly subdued than Griffin Dunne’s incredulous Paul Hackett. Still, the way Kafka plays ridiculous moments so straight-faced brought me to tears on the BART at least once.

When K.’s lawyer embarks on a lengthy monologue on the inner workings of the trial, Kafka declines to give a snapshot of the speech, instead making the reader sit through nearly 10 pages of legal rambling. There was nothing funny about each successive page of jargon, but eventually it was so absurd I just had to laugh. By inciting tedium, Kafka formally elicits in his audience an understanding of the hilarity in K’s powerlessness.

Anyways, I wasn’t surprised to learn that Kafka sometimes laughed so hard when reading drafts to friends that he was unable to finish. He’s just a man who’s really committed to the bit and I commend him for it. Funny guy.

title: From Russia with Love (1963)
content:
**My first post!** Trying this experiment out in a dual effort to

1. learn Claude Code and Github for my internship, and
2. document my thoughts on books, movies, and music.

Very exciting! Since I don't have anything prepared, I'll paste this Letterboxd review from the last movie I watched.

From Russia with Love has Tintin-esque qualities — it's a flamboyant and whimsical international romp filled with quirky gadgets and larger-than-life characters, from a chess-playing spymaster to Bond's affable ally Kerim Bey. At the same time, the colorful constructed sets and vivid film stock, which create visual clarity in a way not unlike Hergé's ligne claire, belie a real, sinister sense of danger rooted in the Cold War tensions of the time. Terence Young blends these tones together, flipping from beautiful location shooting into visceral claustrophobic fights, then back into campy one-liners ("she's had her kicks") in a way that never feels jarring.
